questions.text: Which of the following are not polymer?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Proteins are polymers of amino acids.
Polysaccharides are monomers of saccharide units (monosaccharides).
The structures of lipids vary widely with many containing long hydrocarbon tails or carbon ring structures, but no monomers.
Nucleic acids are polymers of nucleotides .

questions.explanation: Lipids are fatty acid esters of alcohol and related substances. Polysaccharides are polymers of monosaccharides. Proteins are polymers of amino acids, and nucleic acids are polymers of nucleotides.

questions.text: Which of the following is the least likely to be involved in stabilizing the three­ dimensional folding of most proteins?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hydrogen bonds stabilize the tertiary structure of proteins and is a weak one.
Electrostatic interaction stabilize the tertiary structure of proteins and is a weak one.
Hydrophobic interaction stabilize the tertiary structure of proteins and is a weak one.

Protein folding is the process by which a protein structure assumes its functional shape or conformation by allowing interaction between amino acids by various bond formations. All protein molecules are heterogeneous unbranched chains of amino acids. Different types of weak interactions hold the interacting segments together in protein. Hydrophobic interaction, H bonds, and electrostatic interactions are the weak ones and stabilize the tertiary structure of proteins, while ester bonds are covalent bonds and are least likely to be involved in stabilizing the three-dimensional folding of most proteins.

questions.text: Which one of the following biomolecules is correctly characterized?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Lecithin a phosphorylated glyceride found in the cell membrane.
options.explanation: Lecithin is a triglyceride lipid where one fatty acid is replaced by phosphoric acid, which is linked to an additional nitrogenous group called choline. It is a common membrane lipid. It is an amphipathic phospholipid, having both hydrophilic polar and hydrophobic nonpolar groups. The hydrocarbon chains of two fatty acids function as hydrophobic non­polar tails, whereas the phosphate and choline behave as a hydrophilic polar head group of the molecule.

options.text: Palmitic acid ­an unsaturated fatty acid with 18 carbon atoms.
options.explanation: Palmitic acid is a saturated fatty acid (as it does not possess double bonds in its carbon chain) and contains 16 carbon atoms with formula C 16 H 32 O2.

options.text: Adenylic acid adenosine with a glucose phosphate molecule.
options.explanation: Adenylic acid or adenosine monophosphate is a nucleotide formed by the union of adenine (nitrogenous base), ribose (pentose sugar), and phosphate. It is formed through phosphorylation of nucleoside as phosphate combines with sugar molecule at its 5 carbon atom.

options.text: Alanine amino acid­ contains an amino group and an acidic group anywhere in the molecule.
options.explanation: Amino acids are organic acids with a carboxylic group (–COOH) having an amino group (– NH 2) generally attached to a­ carbon or carbon next to a carboxylic group. Alanine is a nonpolar and neutral amino acid having one methyl group and having an amino group attached to the carbon next to the carboxylic group.

questions.explanation: Lecithin is a triglyceride lipid where one fatty acid is replaced by phosphoric acid, which is linked to an additional nitrogenous group called choline. It is a common membrane lipid. It is an amphipathic phospholipid, having both hydrophilic polar and hydrophobic nonpolar groups. The hydrocarbon chains of two fatty acids function as hydrophobic non­polar tails, whereas the phosphate and choline behave as a hydrophilic polar head group of the molecule. Palmitic acid is a saturated fatty acid (as it does not possess double bonds in its carbon chain) and contains 16 carbon atoms with formula C 16 H 32 O2. Adenylic acid or adenosine monophosphate is a nucleotide formed by the union of adenine (nitrogenous base), ribose (pentose sugar), and phosphate. It is formed through phosphorylation of nucleoside as phosphate combines with sugar molecule at its 5 carbon atom. Amino acids are organic acids with a carboxylic group (–COOH) having an amino group (– NH 2) generally attached to a­ carbon or carbon next to a carboxylic group. Alanine is a nonpolar and neutral amino acid having one methyl group and having an amino group attached to the carbon next to the carboxylic group.

questions.text: Carbohydrates are commonly found as starch in plants storage organs. Which of the following five properties of starch make it useful as a storage material? (1) Easily translocated (2) Chemically non­reactive (3) Easily digested by animals (4) Osmotically inactive (5) Synthesized during photosynthesis The useful properties are?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: 1,3 and 5 properties donot tell about its storage function.
1 and 5 properties are irrelevant to its storage function.
3 property doenot tell anything about its storage function.

Starch is the major storage carbohydrate of plants. In most plant species it is accumulated in the chloroplast of leaves, whereas in storage organs it accumulates in the amyloplast as reserve starch. It is the osmotically inactive form of photosynthetic product and is a hexosan polysaccharide made of many glucose units, so it is chemically non-reactive.

questions.explanation: Starch is the major storage carbohydrate of plants. In most plant species it is accumulated in the chloroplast of leaves, whereas in storage organs it accumulates in the amyloplast as reserve starch. It is the osmotically inactive form of photosynthetic product and is a hexosan polysaccharide made of a large number of glucose units so it is chemically non-reactive.

questions.text: The major role of minor elements inside living organisms is to act as?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Minor elements are those which are required in the quantity of less than milligram/gram of dry matter, but they are essential for proper growth and development of an organism e.g., Cl, Mn, B, Zn, Cu, Mo, etc. These elements work as non­protein cofactor in enzymes e.g., Zn, Cu, etc. They also take part in oxidation-reduction reactions, e.g., Cu, with variable valency. Chloride ion enhances the activity of salivary amylase. Zinc is required for the activity of carbonic anhydrase and alcohol dehydrogenase, etc.

questions.text: Lipids are insoluble in water because lipid molecules are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lipids are insoluble because of their hydrophobic property (water-repellent). Hydrophillic means water loving and hydrophilic substances are soluble in water.

Lipid molecules are insoluble or sparingly soluble in water but are freely soluble in organic solvents like ether, alcohol, and benzene. The insolubility of lipids in water is due to the fact that the polar groups they contain are much smaller than their nonpolar portions. The nonpolar chains are long, complex hydrophobic hydrocarbon chains. If shaken in water, lipids often form small droplets or micelles. The complex created is called emulsions. These nonpolar proteins give them water-repellent or hydrophobic properties.
They are insoluble in water because of hydrophobic property. Being neutral has nothing to do with solubility.
Amino acids exist as zwiter ions.

questions.text: Which of the following is a reducing sugar?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: All monosaccharides are reducing sugars.
These are not pure carbohydrates. Pure carbohydrates like monosaccharides, oligosaccharides, and polysaccharides are classified as reducing and non-reducing sugar.
These are not pure carbohydrates. Pure carbohydrates like monosaccharides, oligosaccharides, and polysaccharides are classified as reducing and non-reducing sugar.
Sucrose is non-reducing sugar.

questions.explanation: All those sugars which have free aldehyde or ketone group are called reducing sugars. These are able to reduce cupric ions (Cu+2) into cuprous ions (Cu+). Sucrose and starch are non-­reducing sugars.

Sucrose is composed of glucose and fructose.

questions.text: What are the most diverse molecules in the cell?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lipids are a diverse group of molecules in cells, including fats, phospholipids, and steroids, but their diversity is not as extensive as that of proteins. Proteins are involved in a wide range of functions and structures in the cell, making them more diverse in terms of their functions and structures.
Mineral salts, such as sodium, potassium, calcium, and others, are important for cellular processes, but they are relatively simple in terms of molecular diversity compared to proteins.

Proteins are incredibly diverse molecules in cells. They have a wide variety of functions, including enzymes that catalyze chemical reactions, structural proteins that provide cellular support, transport proteins, antibodies, and more. Proteins are made up of various sequences of amino acids, allowing for a vast number of possible protein structures and functions.

Carbohydrates are important for energy storage and cell-cell recognition, but they are not as diverse in terms of molecular structures as proteins. Proteins can have complex three-dimensional structures and a wider range of functions.

questions.explanation: Proteins show enormous diversity because of different proportions and sequences of twenty amino acids within the protein molecule. A large number of permutations and combinations of these amino acids are responsible for the unlimited variety of proteins. Proteins are the most abundant and most varied of the macromolecules having one or more polypeptides (chains of amino acids). The proteins constitute almost 50% of the total dry weight of the cell. Proteins may be simple or conjugated. Among conjugated, proteins may be phosphoprotein, glycoprotein, nucleoprotein, chromoprotein, lipoprotein, flavoprotein, metalloprotein, etc. Functionally proteins may be structural protein, enzymes, hormones, respiratory pigment, etc.

questions.text: Which purine base is found in RNA?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Thymine is a pyrimidine base and is not found in RNA. It is one of the four nitrogenous bases in DNA, where it pairs with adenine (a purine).
Uracil is a pyrimidine base and is indeed found in RNA. Uracil pairs with adenine in RNA, just as thymine pairs with adenine in DNA.
Cytosine is a pyrimidine base found in both RNA and DNA. In RNA, cytosine can base-pair with guanine.
Guanine is a purine base found in both RNA and DNA. In RNA, guanine pairs with cytosine.

questions.explanation: The bases are of two types­: purines and pyrimidines. The purine derivatives adenine (A) and guanine (G) are double ring structures whereas pyrimidine derivatives thymine, cytosine, and uracil are single ring structures. Thymine (T) and cytosine (C) are found in DNA and cytosine (C) and uracil (U) is found in RNA. Pneumonic for remembering purines: Pure As Gold.

questions.text: Which of the following nucleotide sequence contain 4 pyrimidine bases?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Pyrimidine bases are of three types, i.e., cytosine (C), thymine (T) and uracil (U). Thymine is only found in DNA, while the uracil is only found in RNA. Thus, this option is incorrect.
Pyrimidine bases are of three types, i.e., cytosine (C), thymine (T) and uracil (U). Thymine is only found in DNA, while the uracil is only found in RNA. Thus, this option is correct.
Pyrimidine bases are of three types, i.e., cytosine (C), thymine (T) and uracil (U). Thymine is only found in DNA, while the uracil is only found in RNA. Thus, this option is incorrect.
Pyrimidine bases are of three types, i.e., cytosine (C), thymine (T) and uracil (U). Thymine is only found in DNA, while the uracil is only found in RNA. Thus, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: In DNA and RNA, these bases form hydrogen bonds with their complementary purines. Thus, in DNA, the purines adenine (A) and guanine (G) pair up with the pyrimidines thymine (T) and cytosine (C), respectively.Normally the Nucleotides contain Nitrogenous base,pentose sugar,phosphate groups.

questions.text: What are the six elements that make up 99% of all elements found in a living system?

questions.text: What is glycogen, a polymer of?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Galactose is a monosaccharide, not a polymer. It's one of the components of lactose, a disaccharide found in milk.

Glucose is the correct answer. Glycogen is a polysaccharide consisting of many glucose molecules linked together in a branched structure. It serves as a storage form of energy in animals, particularly in the liver and muscles.
Fructose is another monosaccharide, not a polymer. It's commonly found in fruits and is one of the components of sucrose.
Sucrose is a disaccharide composed of glucose and fructose molecules, not a polymer like glycogen.

questions.explanation: Glycogen (animal starch) is a polysaccharide. It is a highly branched polymer of glucose occurring in animal tissues, especially in liver and muscle cells. It is the major store of carbohydrate energy in animal cells.

questions.text: Amino acids are mostly synthesized from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This term doesn't accurately describe a source for amino acid synthesis. Amino acids are not synthesized from "mineral cells."

Amino acids are not typically synthesized from fatty acids. While some fatty acids can be converted into other compounds through various metabolic pathways, they are not a direct precursor to amino acids.
Volatile acids are not a source of amino acid synthesis. Amino acids are typically synthesized from precursor molecules within metabolic pathways.

This is the correct answer. Amino acids are synthesized through various metabolic pathways, and a-ketoglutaric acid is an intermediate molecule in the citric acid cycle (Krebs cycle) that can serve as a precursor for the synthesis of certain amino acids.

questions.explanation: Amino acids are mostly synthesized from a­-ketoglutaric acid. These are the precursors of amino acids. A five-carbon compound formed during Krebs’ cycle is an a­-ketoglutaric acid which is the first dicarboxylic acid formed. Pyruvic acid is converted into alanine, a­ketoglutaric acid into glutamic acid and OAA into aspartic acid. The polymerization of such amino acids results in the formation of proteins.

questions.text: Which is distributed more widely in a cell?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: DNA is primarily found in the cell's nucleus (in eukaryotic cells) or nucleoid region (in prokaryotic cells). It contains genetic information and is responsible for encoding instructions for the cell's functions.

RNA is distributed more widely throughout the cell. It can be found in the nucleus, cytoplasm, and even in specific organelles like the ribosomes and mitochondria. RNA plays a crucial role in various cellular processes, including protein.

Chloroplasts are specialized organelles found in plant cells and some algae. They contain chlorophyll and are primarily involved in photosynthesis. Chloroplasts are not distributed widely throughout the cell but are instead concentrated in the chloroplasts of plant cells.

Phagosomes are membrane-bound vesicles formed during the process of phagocytosis, where cells engulf and digest particles like bacteria or cellular debris. They are not widely distributed throughout the cell, but form as needed during this specific cellular process.

questions.explanation: RNA occurs in the nucleus as well as in the cytoplasm of the eukaryotic cells and in a prokaryotic cell, it is found in the cytoplasm. DNA is found in the nucleus, mitochondria, and chloroplast. Chloroplast and phagosomes are found only in the cytoplasm.

This option is correct, as the human body contains 65 – 70% water.

questions.explanation: Water is the most abundant substance of living beings. The water content of actively living cells varies between 60–95%. In human beings, maximum water content is found in the embryo (90 – 95%). Water content decreases to 65-70% thereafter in adults.

questions.text: What are the components of a nucleotide?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option incorrectly separates the nitrogen bases into purine and pyrimidine. In reality, both purines (like adenine and guanine) and pyrimidines (like cytosine, thymine in DNA, or uracil in RNA) are types of nitrogen bases found in nucleotides. So, this option is not accurate.

While it mentions the correct nitrogen base type (purine), it's still incorrect because it doesn't include pyrimidines. Nucleotides can contain either purine or pyrimidine nitrogen bases, not both in the same nucleotide.

This is the correct answer. Nucleotides consist of a nitrogen base (either purine or pyrimidine), a sugar (deoxyribose in DNA or ribose in RNA), and a phosphate group. These three components make up the structure of nucleotides.
This option incorrectly separates the nitrogen bases into pyrimidine only. Nucleotides can contain either purine or pyrimidine nitrogen bases, so this option is not accurate.

questions.explanation: A nucleotide is an organic compound consisting of a nitrogen­ containing purine or pyrimidine base linked to a sugar (ribose or deoxyribose) and a phosphate group.

questions.text: A segment of DNA has 120 adenine and 120 cytosine bases. The total number of nucleotides present in the segment is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect as the total number of nucleotides present in the segment is 480.
This option is incorrect as the total number of nucleotides present in the segment is 480.
This option is incorrect as the total number of nucleotides present in the segment is 480.

This option is correct. According to Chargaff’s rules, the amount of adenine is always equal to that of thymine, and the amount of guanine is always equal to that of cytosine i.e.,A = T (120) and G = C (120), therefore, the total no. of nucleotides would be 120 × 4 = 480.

questions.explanation: According to Chargaff’s rules, the amount of adenine is always equal to that of thymine, and the amount of guanine is always equal to that of cytosine i.e.,A = T (120) and G = C (120), therefore, the total no. of nucleotides would be 120 × 4 = 480.

questions.text: The basic unit of nucleic acid is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A pentose sugar, such as ribose or deoxyribose, is one of the components of a nucleotide, but it is not the basic unit of nucleic acids on its own.

The nucleoid is a region within prokaryotic cells (like bacteria) where the genetic material, typically in the form of a single circular DNA molecule, is located. It is not the basic unit of nucleic acids.

A nucleoside is a molecule that consists of a nitrogenous base and a pentose sugar, but it lacks the phosphate group found in nucleotides. Nucleosides are not the basic unit of nucleic acids, as they do not have the full structure required for the formation of DNA or RNA.

This is the correct answer. A nucleotide is the fundamental unit of nucleic acids, such as DNA and RNA. It consists of three main components: a nitrogenous base (either a purine or a pyrimidine), a pentose sugar (either ribose in RNA or deoxyribose in DNA), and a phosphate group. Nucleotides link together to form the long chains of DNA or RNA.

questions.explanation: The nucleic acids (DNA and RNA) are molecules having complex structures and very high molecular weights. A nucleic acid is composed of many nucleotide molecules joined into a linear, unbranched chain. A nucleotide is an organic compound consisting of a nitrogen­ containing purine or pyrimidine base linked to a sugar (ribose or deoxyribose) and a phosphate group.

questions.text: In the double helix of DNA, the two DNA strands are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect as in the double helix of DNA, the two DNA strands are coiled around a common axis. DNA is made of two polynucleotide chains or strands. The two strands are coiled round each other in the form of a double helix. Coiling of two strands is opposite, i.e., they are coiled antiparallel to each other. The two chains are held together by weak bonds (hydrogen bonds).

double helix of DNA, the two DNA strands are coiled around a common axis. DNA is made of two polynucleotide chains or strands. The two strands are coiled round each other in the form of a double helix. Coiling of two strands is opposite i.e. they are coiled antiparallel to each other. The two chains are held together by weak bonds (hydrogen bonds).

This option is incorrect as in the double helix of DNA, the two DNA strands are coiled around a common axis.

questions.explanation: According to the Watson-­Crick model, the DNA molecule consists of two long, parallel chains which are joined together by short crossbars at regular intervals. The two chains are spirally coiled around a common axis in a regular manner to form a right-handed double helix.

questions.text: What is the most abundant carbohydrate in nature?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cellulose is the correct answer. It is a complex carbohydrate found in the cell walls of plants and is the primary structural component of plant cell walls. It is a major component of plant biomass and one of the most abundant organic compounds on Earth.

Starch is a storage carbohydrate found in plants, consisting of glucose molecules linked together. While it is abundant in plants and serves as a storage form of energy, it is not as prevalent in nature as cellulose.
Fructose is a simple sugar (monosaccharide) found in fruits and honey, but it is not the most abundant carbohydrate in nature.
Glycogen is a storage carbohydrate found in animals, including humans, and is stored in the liver and muscles. It is not as abundant in nature as cellulose.

questions.explanation: A type of carbohydrate, cellulose contains chains of glucose rings, providing strength and rigidity. Cellulose forms the cell walls of plants and is the primary constituent of wood, making this organic compound the most abundant one on the surface of the Earth.

questions.text: Which of the following does not yield glucose on complete hydrolysis?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Starch is a carbohydrate that is commonly found in many plant-based foods and serves as a significant source of energy for humans and animals. It is composed of long chains of glucose molecules linked together. Starch is a polysaccharide, which means it consists of multiple sugar units bonded together. Starch is a vital component in the diet of many cultures around the world and is found in foods such as potatoes, rice, wheat, maize (corn), and other grains. These foods are staples in many diets and provide a major source of calories and energy for humans. Starch can be broken down into simpler sugars, such as glucose, through the process of digestion.

Glycogen is a complex carbohydrate and a form of stored energy in animals, including humans. It is often referred to as animal starch due to its structural similarity to starch in plants. Glycogen serves as a readily available and quickly mobilized source of glucose, which is essential for maintaining energy levels and regulating blood sugar.: In the human body, glycogen is primarily stored in the liver and muscles. The liver stores glycogen to maintain blood glucose levels and provide energy between meals, while muscles store glycogen for local energy use during physical activity.Glycogen is a polysaccharide made up of glucose molecules linked together through α-1,4-glycosidic bonds, similar to the structure of starch.

Unlike starch and glycogen, which are easily broken down by digestive enzymes in animals and humans, cellulose cannot be digested by most animals, including humans, due to the β-1,4-glycosidic bonds that are resistant to our digestive enzymes.is a complex carbohydrate and a fundamental structural component of plant cell walls. It is the most abundant organic compound on Earth, and it provides strength, rigidity, and stability to plant cells. Cellulose is a linear polysaccharide composed of repeating glucose units linked together by β-1,4-glycosidic bonds.

Chitin is a natural biopolymer that is similar in structure to cellulose, but it is primarily found in the exoskeletons of arthropods, such as insects, crustaceans (like shrimp and crabs), and some other invertebrates like mollusks and fungi. Chitin is a long-chain polymer composed of N-acetyl glucosamine (GlcNAc) molecules linked together by β-1,4-glycosidic bonds, similar to cellulose. This arrangement of glucose-like units gives chitin its rigidity and strength. In arthropods like insects and crustaceans, chitin is a major component of their exoskeletons. It provides support and protection, allowing these animals to maintain their body shape and protect themselves from environmental threats.

questions.explanation: Chitin is an Nz-containing polysaccharide and it will yield N-acetyl-D-glucosamine on hydrolysis while starch and glycogen will yield a-D glucose monomers and Cellulose will yield B-D glucose monomers on hydrolysis.

questions.text: Which sugar does glucose combine with to give Lactose (milk sugar) ?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While glucose is one of the components of lactose, it combines with galactose to form lactose, not with itself.
Fructose is a different sugar and is not one of the components of lactose.
This is the correct answer. Lactose is made up of glucose and galactose molecules. When these two sugars combine, they form lactose, which is commonly found in milk.
Mannose is a different sugar and is not one of the components of lactose.

questions.explanation: Glucose and galactose combine to give lactose-the milk sugar. Glucose and glucose give maltose, while glucose and fructose give sucrose.

questions.text: How many water molecules will be released upon the formation of a trisaccharide?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Since, upon the formation of a trisaccharide, two water molecules will be released, this option is incorrect.

The formation of a trisaccharide involves the linking of three monosaccharide molecules (simple sugars) through glycosidic bonds. In the process of forming a glycosidic bond, a water molecule (H2O) is released as a byproduct. The general reaction for the formation of a trisaccharide can be represented as follows: Monosaccharide 1 + Monosaccharide 2 + Monosaccharide 3 → Trisaccharide + 3H2O. As we can see from the reaction, for each glycosidic bond formed between two monosaccharides, one water molecule is released. Since a trisaccharide involves the formation of two glycosidic bonds, two water molecules are released in the process. Therefore, upon the formation of a trisaccharide, two water molecules will be released.
Since, upon the formation of a trisaccharide, two water molecules will be released, this option is incorrect.
Since, upon the formation of a trisaccharide, two water molecules will be released, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Upon the formation of a trisaccharide, two water molecules will be released.

This option is correct, as 4 carbon atoms are present inside the ring of fructose.

questions.text: Which of the following polysaccharides is present in human muscles abundantly?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Myoglobin is a protein, not a polysaccharide. It's found in muscle tissues and is responsible for oxygen storage.
Collagen is a protein found in connective tissues like tendons, ligaments, and skin. It's not a polysaccharide.
Actin and myosin are also proteins, not polysaccharides. They are vital for muscle contraction.
Glycogen is the correct answer. It is a polysaccharide and serves as the primary storage form of glucose in human muscles and the liver. It can be broken down into glucose when the body needs energy.

questions.text: Pick out the odd one:

options.explanation: Cellulose is a polysaccharide found in plant cell walls and provides structural support. It's composed of glucose units linked together.
Galactose is a monosaccharide, a single sugar molecule. It's commonly found as a component of lactose (milk sugar) and other carbohydrates.
Agar is a polysaccharide extracted from seaweed and is used in microbiological culture media and as a thickening agent in food.
Pectin is a polysaccharide found in the cell walls of fruits and vegetables. It's used as a gelling agent in making jams and jellies.

questions.explanation: Cellulose, agar and pectin are examples of polysaccharides but galactose is an example of a monosaccharide.

The 1st carbon is part of the fructose ring. However, the 6th carbon is outside the ring and forms an aldehyde group (CHO). This is the correct answer.

questions.explanation: Fructose can form a five cornered ring structure called fructofuranose, having four carbons and an oxygen atom within the ring while the first and last carbons remain outside the ring, i.e., 1st carbon and 6th carbon.

questions.text: Which of the following sugar is most sweet?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Glucose is less sweet than sucrose. It's often described as having a sweetness value of around 0.7 to 0.8 compared to sucrose.
Sucrose is the reference point with a sweetness value of 1.0. It's a common sweetener found in table sugar.
Fructose is sweeter than sucrose. It's generally considered to be about 1.2 to 1.8 times sweeter than sucrose.
Maltose is less sweet than sucrose, with a sweetness value typically around 0.3 to 0.5 compared to sucrose.

questions.explanation: Fructose has the ketone functional group which gives it a sweet taste when dissolved in water.

questions.text: Which of the following is a non-reducing disaccharide sugar?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mannose is a monosaccharide, not a disaccharide, so it doesn't fit the category of non-reducing disaccharides.
Maltose is a reducing disaccharide. It's composed of two glucose units linked together, and it has a free reducing sugar group.

Sucrose is a non-reducing disaccharide. It's composed of one glucose unit and one fructose unit linked together. The glycosidic bond between them prevents the formation of a free reducing sugar group.
Lactose is a reducing disaccharide. It's made up of one glucose unit and one galactose unit linked together, and it has a free reducing sugar group.

questions.explanation: The reducing group in sucrose is used in the glycosidic bond formation due to which it is non-reducing in nature. On the other hand, mannose, maltose and Lactose are all reducing sugars.

questions.text: Which of the following sugar is mainly present in human blood?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Glucose is the primary sugar found in human blood and is used by our cells for energy. It's a monosaccharide and is often referred to as blood sugar.
Sucrose is a disaccharide composed of glucose and fructose. It's not typically found in human blood as it needs to be broken down into its constituent monosaccharides before it can be absorbed.
Fructose is another monosaccharide, but it's not the main sugar in human blood. It is absorbed and metabolized differently from glucose.
Mannose is a monosaccharide, and it is not the primary sugar present in human blood.

questions.explanation: Glucose is required for essential processes such as respiration, hence it is the main sugar present in blood. In non-diabetic individuals, the concentration of glucose in the blood is about 0.08%

questions.text: Which of the following is an amino acid and part of phospholipids?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Serine is an amino acid and is indeed a component of phospholipids. It plays a crucial role in the structure of phosphatidylserine, a type of phospholipid found in cell membranes.

Choline is not an amino acid, but it is a crucial component of phospholipids. It's a quaternary ammonium salt containing a hydrophilic head, making it important for the structure of phosphatidylcholine, a common phospholipid in cell membranes.
Ethanolamine is not an amino acid, but like choline, it is a vital part of phospholipids. It contributes to the structure of phosphatidylethanolamine, another phospholipid in cell membranes.
Aspartic acid is an amino acid, but is not typically found as a component of phospholipids.

questions.explanation: Serine and aspartic acids are examples of amino acids, while choline and ethanolamine are Nz-containing bases. Serine can be a constituent of phospholipids and can act as a nitrogenous base.

questions.text: What is the myelin sheath of a neuron composed of?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sphingolipids, including sphingomyelin, are a class of lipids that make up a significant part of the myelin sheath. They provide structural support and insulation for nerve fibers.
Choline is not a major component of the myelin sheath. While it is important for various cellular functions, it is not a primary structural component of myelin.
Ethanolamine is not a primary component of the myelin sheath either. Like choline, it has other roles in cell membranes but is not a key structural element of myelin.
Waxes are not typically found in the myelin sheath of neurons. Myelin is primarily a lipid-rich substance composed of sphingolipids and proteins.

questions.explanation: The myelin sheath of neurons is composed of sphingolipids. Ethanolamine and choline are nitrogenous bases found in phospholipids while axes are abundantly found in protective covering of fruits and leaves.

questions.text: Which of the following does not form a biopolymer?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Amino acids are the monomers that make up proteins. Proteins are biopolymers composed of amino acid chains linked together by peptide bonds.
Fatty acids can be part of lipids, but they do not typically form biopolymers. Lipids are not considered polymers because they are not built from repeating monomers.
Nucleotides are the monomers that make up nucleic acids (DNA and RNA). Nucleic acids are biopolymers composed of long chains of nucleotide units linked together.

Monosaccharides are simple sugars and are the monomers that make up carbohydrates. Carbohydrates, including complex polysaccharides like starch and cellulose, are biopolymers composed of repeating monosaccharide units.

questions.explanation: Amino acids join to form proteins. Nucleotides join to form nucleic acid, while monosaccharides join to form polysaccharides.

questions.text: Which of the following amino acids is next to glycine with respect to molecular structure?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Alanine is structurally similar to glycine, with a methyl group (-CH3) replacing the hydrogen atom in glycine. So, alanine is next to glycine in terms of molecular structure.

Serine has a more complex structure compared to glycine and alanine. It has a hydroxyl (-OH) group in addition to a methyl group. Therefore, serine is not next to glycine in terms of molecular structure.
Ethanolamine is not an amino acid; it's an organic compound containing an amino group (-NH2), but it doesn't have the typical amino acid structure with a carboxyl group (-COOH).
Aspartic acid is also not next to glycine in terms of molecular structure. It has a carboxyl group and a side chain, making its structure more complex than glycine and alanine.

questions.explanation: The simplest amino acid is glycine in which R is a 'H' atom. Alanine is next to glycine which contains -CH3 as its R-group.

questions.text: A globular protein consisting of more than one polypeptide chains belongs to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The primary structure of a protein refers to the linear sequence of amino acids in a single polypeptide chain. It is the most basic level of protein structure and is determined by the peptide bonds between amino acids.

The secondary structure involves the folding or coiling of a single polypeptide chain into specific shapes such as alpha-helices or beta-sheets. It is stabilized by hydrogen bonds between amino acids within the same chain.

The tertiary structure is the three-dimensional arrangement of a single polypeptide chain, including the folding, bending, and twisting of the chain. It is stabilized by various interactions such as hydrogen bonds, disulfide bonds, hydrophobic interactions, and ionic interactions.

The quaternary structure involves the arrangement of multiple polypeptide chains (subunits) in a protein complex. Proteins with quaternary structure are composed of two or more individual polypeptide chains that come together to form a functional unit. Interactions between these subunits, such as non-covalent interactions and disulfide bonds, contribute to the stability and function of the complex.

questions.explanation: In many complex proteins, polypeptide tertiary chains are aggregated and held together by hydrophobic interactions, hydrogen and ionic bonds. This specific arrangement is the quaternary structure. Quaternary structure refers to when 2 or more polypeptide chains are joined together in a protein.

questions.text: All the following are examples of fibrous proteins, except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Silk fiber is a natural protein fiber produced by certain insects, primarily the silkworm. It is highly valued for its luxurious feel, sheen, and strength, making it a prized material in the textile industry. Silk is composed mainly of two proteins, fibroin, and sericin. Fibroin is the core protein that makes up the silk fibers and gives them their strength and smooth texture. Sericin is a gummy substance that holds the fibers together within the cocoon.

Keratin is a fibrous structural protein that is the primary constituent of hair, nails, feathers, hooves, and the outer layer of skin in humans and many other animals. It is exceptionally strong and provides mechanical strength and protection to these biological structures. Keratin is made up of long chains of amino acids, particularly cysteine, which forms disulfide bridges (sulfur-sulfur bonds) between the protein chains. These disulfide bridges are responsible for the protein's strength and resilience.

Myoglobin is a protein found in muscle tissue, particularly in the skeletal and cardiac muscles of vertebrates, including humans. It plays a crucial role in the storage and transport of oxygen within muscle cells. Myoglobin is a globular protein composed of a single polypeptide chain folded into a compact structure. It contains a heme group, which is the site where oxygen molecules bind. The heme group gives myoglobin its reddish color.

Fibrin is a fibrous, insoluble protein that plays a crucial role in the blood clotting process, also known as coagulation. It is essential for the formation of blood clots, which help to stop bleeding when blood vessels are damaged. Fibrin is a fibrous protein formed by the polymerization of fibrinogen molecules. The polymerization process creates a mesh-like network of fibrin strands, which trap blood cells to form a stable blood clot.

questions.explanation: Silk fiber, keratin and fibrin are examples of fibrous proteins while myoglobin has a tertiary structure and is a typical water soluble globular protein found in muscles.

questions.text: Which of the following is a complex of globular protein with non-proteinaceous material?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Collagen is the most abundant protein in the human body and a critical component of connective tissues, providing structural support and strength to various organs, skin, tendons, ligaments, and more. Collagen is a fibrous protein characterized by a triple helix structure. It is composed of three polypeptide chains (alpha chains) that are twisted together like a rope.

Albumin is a water-soluble protein found in the blood plasma of humans and many other animals. It serves several important functions in the body, primarily related to regulating blood volume and maintaining proper distribution of substances in the bloodstream. Albumin is one of the most abundant proteins in blood plasma, accounting for approximately 60% of the total protein content in the blood. It is synthesized primarily by the liver.

Fibrinogen is a soluble protein found in the blood and is a key component of the blood clotting system. It plays a crucial role in the formation of blood clots, a process known as coagulation. The conversion of fibrinogen into fibrin is catalyzed by the enzyme thrombin. Thrombin cleaves fibrinogen, removing small peptides, and causes the remaining fibrin molecules to form long, insoluble fibers. These fibers create a mesh-like network that traps blood cells to form a stable clot.

Hemoglobin (or hemoglobin) is a protein found in red blood cells (erythrocytes) and is essential for the transportation of oxygen from the lungs to the body's tissues and the return of carbon dioxide from the tissues to the lungs for exhalation. When hemoglobin is exposed to oxygen in the lungs, it binds to the oxygen molecules, forming oxyhemoglobin. Oxyhemoglobin is a bright red color and carries oxygen-rich blood from the lungs to the body's tissues.

questions.explanation: Hemoglobin consists of 4 polypeptide chains forming a quaternary structure, and 4 haem groups containing 4 iron atoms as a non-protein part of the molecule.

questions.text: Which one of the following is a non-protein group covalently bonded to a few respiratory enzymes, among others?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: NAD is a coenzyme involved in redox reactions. It exists in two forms, NAD+ (oxidized) and NADH (reduced). NAD+ accepts electrons during catabolic reactions, while NADH donates electrons during anabolic reactions.

Flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD) is a non-protein group that is covalently bonded to a few respiratory enzymes, among others. FAD is a redox-active coenzyme that plays a crucial role in various metabolic reactions, particularly cellular respiration. It acts as a carrier of electrons and hydrogen atoms during oxidative reactions. FAD is derived from the vitamin riboflavin (vitamin B2) and consists of a flavin mononucleotide (FMN) molecule attached to an adenosine diphosphate (ADP) moiety. It can be covalently bound to certain enzymes, serving as a prosthetic group. In respiratory enzymes, such as flavoproteins, FAD is covalently attached to the enzyme's active site and participates in redox reactions, transferring electrons and hydrogen atoms between substrates.

NADP is involved in redox reactions. It exists in two forms, NADP+ (oxidized) and NADPH (reduced). NADP+ and NADPH are often associated with anabolic reactions, such as those occurring in biosynthetic pathways.

options.text: All of the above options are correct.
options.explanation: Only option B is correct; Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD) is a non-protein group that is covalently bonded to a few respiratory enzymes, among others.FAD is a redox-active coenzyme that plays a crucial role in various metabolic reactions, particularly cellular respiration. It acts as a carrier of electrons and hydrogen atoms during oxidative reactions. FAD is derived from the vitamin riboflavin (vitamin B2) and consists of a flavin mononucleotide (FMN) molecule attached to an adenosine diphosphate (ADP) moiety. It can be covalently bound to certain enzymes, serving as a prosthetic group. In respiratory enzymes, such as flavoproteins, FAD is covalently attached to the enzyme's active site and participates in redox reactions, transferring electrons and hydrogen atoms between substrates. Therefore, FAD is a non-protein group covalently bonded to a few respiratory enzymes, among others, and functions as a vital cofactor for their catalytic activity.

questions.text: Haemoglobin possesses:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A linear polypeptide with a specific sequence and number of amino acids is called primary structure. It is shown by all proteins at the time of their synthesis on ribosomal surface. After synthesis a protein does not remain in its primary structure but can be changed into some other structural conformations (particular form, shape or structure).

A helical (a-helix) or flattened sheets (13-pleated sheet) like structures which are established by H-bonding between opposite charge bearing groups of different amino acids are called secondary structures.
In some proteins the linear polypeptide is changed into a-helix, then ahelix fold again and again by ionic bonds and disulfide bridges to form a globular shaped structure, the tertiary structure.
Some proteins exist in very complex structure in which more than one globule is attached together by hydrophobic interaction. Such structures are called quaternary structures.

questions.text: _ are defensive proteins.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: An antigen is a molecule or molecular structure that is recognized by the immune system as foreign or non-self, and it elicits an immune response. Antigens on the surface of bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites can trigger an immune response when the immune system detects them as foreign invaders.

Antibodies, also known as immunoglobulins, are Y-shaped proteins produced by the immune system in response to the presence of antigens, such as bacteria, viruses, and other foreign substances. Antibodies play a critical role in the immune response, as they help the body identify and neutralize these foreign invaders.

Vaccines are biological products designed to stimulate an individual's immune system and provide protection against specific infectious diseases. They work by introducing a harmless form of an antigen, such as a weakened or inactivated pathogen (like a virus or bacterium) or a component of the pathogen (like a protein), into the body. This exposure triggers an immune response without causing the actual disease. The immune system responds by producing antibodies and memory cells, which "remember" how to combat the specific pathogen if the individual is later exposed to the real, potentially harmful version of the pathogen.

Fibrinogen is a glycoprotein in the blood that plays a crucial role in the blood clotting process, also known as coagulation. It is one of the several clotting factors, or coagulation proteins, involved in the complex cascade of events that occurs when a blood vessel is injured, leading to the formation of a blood clot.

questions.explanation: Antibodies are also called immunoglobulins, they are the part of the immune system and protect the body against invading pathogens.

questions.text: Proteins of hair, horns, feathers, and other skin parts is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Storage proteins are a class of proteins that are synthesized by living organisms and serve as a reservoir of amino acids or other essential nutrients. These proteins are typically stored in specialized cells or tissues, and their primary function is to provide a source of essential nutrients when neededIn animals, storage proteins can be found in various tissues, with the liver being a common site for the storage of certain proteins.

Enzymatic proteins, often simply referred to as enzymes, are a specialized class of proteins that play a fundamental role in biological processes. Enzymes act as biological catalysts, speeding up chemical reactions in living organisms without being consumed or permanently altered in the process. These reactions are essential for various cellular functions, including metabolism, digestion, and signal transduction.

Structural proteins are a class of proteins that provide physical support and maintain the structural integrity of cells, tissues, and organisms. They contribute to the form, shape, and mechanical strength of various biological structures.

Hormonal proteins, also known as peptide hormones, are a class of proteins that act as signaling molecules in the endocrine system of organisms. These proteins play a vital role in regulating various physiological processes by transmitting information from one part of the body to another. Hormonal proteins are typically produced and secreted by specialized endocrine glands or cells and travel through the bloodstream to target tissues or organs, where they exert their effects.

options.explanation: Carbohydrates are one of the major classes of macromolecules, and they are a fundamental source of energy for living organisms. They are composed of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen atoms, and they serve various essential functions in both plants and animals. Carbohydrates are commonly found in a variety of foods, including grains, fruits, vegetables, and dairy products.Carbohydrates are the primary source of energy for the body. When consumed, they are broken down into glucose (sugar), which can be used by cells for fuel. Excess glucose can be stored as glycogen in the liver and muscles for later use.

Proteins are large, complex macromolecules made up of amino acids, and they are essential to the structure and function of all living cells. They are involved in a wide range of biological processes and perform numerous critical roles in the human body.Proteins are composed of amino acids, which are the building blocks of these macromolecules. There are 20 standard amino acids that can be combined in various sequences to create a wide array of proteins.

Lipids are a diverse class of organic molecules that are essential to life and play various important roles in living organisms. Unlike carbohydrates and proteins, lipids are not defined by a specific chemical structure, but rather by their hydrophobic (water-repelling) properties.Lipids are hydrophobic molecules, which means they do not readily dissolve in water but are soluble in nonpolar solvents, such as organic solvents like ether, chloroform, and oils.

Nucleic acids are one of the two major classes of macromolecules (the other being proteins) and are essential to the storage and transmission of genetic information in living organisms. They come in two primary forms: deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) and ribonucleic acid (RNA).

It is often referred to as animal starch because of its similarity to starch, which is the storage form of carbohydrates in plants. Glycogen is primarily stored in the liver and muscles, and it serves as a readily available source of glucose for the body's energy needs. Glycogen is a polysaccharide composed of many glucose molecules linked together in a branched structure. It is a highly branched molecule, which allows for quick and efficient breakdown when the body requires glucose.

Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is a molecule that serves as the primary energy currency in cells. It is a nucleotide that plays a crucial role in the transfer and storage of energy for various cellular processes. ATP is composed of three phosphate groups, a ribose sugar molecule, and an adenine base. The three phosphate groups are linked together, and the energy stored in the high-energy phosphate bonds is what makes ATP an energy carrier. ATP is often referred to as the “energy currency” of the cell because it stores and releases energy to power various cellular activities. The energy is stored in the form of chemical potential energy within the phosphate bonds.

Cellulose is a complex carbohydrate and a structural component found in the cell walls of plant cells. It is one of the most abundant organic compounds on Earth and serves a vital role in providing strength and rigidity to plant structures.Cellulose is a linear polymer made up of repeating units of glucose, linked together by β-1,4 glycosidic bonds. Unlike starch, another glucose-based polysaccharide, cellulose has a straight-chain structure.

Hemoglobin also plays a crucial role in the transport of carbon dioxide, a waste product of metabolism, from the tissues back to the lungs, where it can be exhaled.Hemoglobin is a tetrameric protein, meaning it is composed of four protein subunits, each of which is known as a globin. In humans, there are four globin chains: two alpha globin chains and two beta globin chains. Each of these globin chains is bound to a heme group.

They are essential for life and the functioning of living organisms.

A nucleoside is a fundamental component of nucleic acids (DNA and RNA) and serves as the structural building block of these important molecules. A nucleoside is formed by the combination of two components: a nitrogenous base and a pentose sugar. Unlike nucleotides, nucleosides do not contain a phosphate group. Nucleosides are essential components in the structure and function of nucleic acids. They serve as the foundation from which nucleotides are built and, ultimately, the genetic information of an organism is stored, transmitted, and processed.

DNA, or deoxyribonucleic acid, is a molecule that carries the genetic instructions necessary for the growth, development, functioning, and reproduction of all known living organisms and many viruses. It is often described as the "blueprint of life." DNA is a long, double-stranded helical molecule made up of four basic building blocks called nucleotides. The structure of DNA is a double helix, with two long chains (strands) of nucleotides running in opposite directions and connected by hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs. There are four types of nucleotides in DNA, each characterized by a nitrogenous base.

RNA, or ribonucleic acid, is a molecule closely related to DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) and plays a crucial role in the transfer of genetic information and protein synthesis in cells. While DNA serves as the primary repository of genetic information, RNA serves as an intermediary that helps translate that information into functional proteins.

options.explanation: The term “dipole nature” typically refers to the property of a molecule having a dipole moment. A dipole moment is a measure of the separation of positive and negative charges within a molecule. It arises when there is an uneven distribution of electrons, causing one part of the molecule to have a partial positive charge (δ+) and another part to have a partial negative charge (δ-). In the field of molecular biology and biochemistry, researchers often use various additives or agents to stabilize proteins, enzymes, or other biomolecules at elevated temperatures. These agents may include osmolytes (such as trehalose or glycine betaine), chaperone proteins, or specific buffer systems that help maintain the structural integrity and activity of biological molecules during processes like PCR (polymerase chain reaction) or other high-temperature assays.

Organic compounds are primarily composed of carbon atoms bonded to hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, and other nonmetals. Carbon forms the backbone of organic molecules, and its ability to form long chains and complex structures is a characteristic feature. Inorganic compounds generally do not contain carbon-hydrogen (C-H) bonds. While some inorganic compounds may contain carbon, they do not typically exhibit the complex carbon-based structures found in organic compounds. The majority of bonds in organic compounds are covalent bonds, where electrons are shared between atoms. Organic compounds tend to have low melting and boiling points, and they are often volatile. Inorganic compounds can exhibit a wide range of bonding types, including covalent, ionic, and metallic bonds. Many inorganic compounds have high melting and boiling points, are not volatile, and are typically solid at room temperature.

In chemistry, a “polar” molecule is one that has an uneven distribution of charge, resulting in a positive pole and a negative pole. This occurs when the electrons within a molecule are not shared equally between the atoms. Common examples of polar molecules include water (H2O) and ammonia (NH3). Bipolar disorder is a mental health condition characterized by extreme mood swings. It involves episodes of depression (low mood) and episodes of mania or hypomania (high mood). These mood swings can be severe and disruptive to a person's life. It's a medical condition that requires diagnosis and treatment by a healthcare professional.

An ionic bond forms when electrons are transferred from one atom to another, resulting in the creation of positively charged ions (cations) and negatively charged ions (anions). A covalent bond forms when atoms share electrons to achieve a more stable electron configuration. In covalent bonds, electrons are not transferred but shared between atoms.

questions.explanation: Water is an excellent solvent for polar substances due to its dipole nature. options.explanation: Globular proteins are a class of proteins characterized by their compact, roughly spherical (globular) three-dimensional shape. These proteins are typically water-soluble and perform a wide range of biological functions within living organisms. The globular shape of these proteins is in contrast to fibrous proteins, which have a long, extended shape and are typically insoluble in water.

Glycoproteins are a class of proteins that have carbohydrate (sugar) molecules covalently attached to them. These carbohydrate chains can be short and simple or long and complex, and they play essential roles in various biological processes. Glycoproteins are found on the surface of cells and are involved in cell-cell recognition, cell adhesion, immune responses, and various other functions.

Fibrous proteins are a class of proteins characterized by their elongated, thread-like (fibrous) shape and high structural stability. These proteins are typically insoluble in water and serve as structural elements in cells and tissues. Unlike globular proteins, which have a compact and globular shape, fibrous proteins have a more elongated and repetitive structure.

options.explanation: Alanine is a non-essential amino acid that plays important roles in the human body, particularly in protein synthesis and energy metabolism. It is one of the 20 standard amino acids that make up proteins. Alanine is an α-amino acid, meaning it has an amino group (-NH2) and a carboxyl group (-COOH) attached to a central carbon atom, along with a hydrogen atom (-H) and a methyl group (-CH3).

Glycine is one of the 20 standard amino acids that serve as the building blocks of proteins in living organisms. It is the simplest of the amino acids in terms of its chemical structure and is non-essential, meaning it can be synthesized by the human body. Glycine is the only amino acid that is not chiral, meaning it doesn't have a stereoisomer. It has a hydrogen atom as its side chain, making it the smallest and simplest amino acid. Its chemical structure consists of an amino group (-NH2), a carboxyl group (-COOH), and a hydrogen atom (-H) attached to a central carbon atom.

Leucine is one of the 20 standard amino acids that are the building blocks of proteins in living organisms. It is an essential amino acid, which means it cannot be synthesized by the human body and must be obtained through the diet. Leucine plays several important roles in protein synthesis, energy production, and overall health.Leucine is an α-amino acid with a chemical structure that includes an amino group (-NH2), a carboxyl group (-COOH), and a side chain containing an isobutyl group. Its chemical abbreviation is "Leu."

Valine is one of the 20 standard amino acids that serve as the building blocks of proteins in living organisms. It is classified as an essential amino acid, meaning it cannot be synthesized by the human body and must be obtained through the diet. Valine, along with leucine and isoleucine, is one of the branched-chain amino acids (BCAAs) and plays important roles in protein synthesis, energy production, and various metabolic processes. Valine is an α-amino acid with a chemical structure that includes an amino group (-NH2), a carboxyl group (-COOH), and a side chain containing an isopropyl group. Its chemical abbreviation is "Val."

It is not commonly used as a source of energy in living organisms, but rather it has important applications in various industries and especially in the field of cosmetics and self-tanning products.

Glucose is a simple sugar and a crucial source of energy for living organisms. Glucose is commonly found in various foods, including carbohydrates such as sugars and starches, and it is also produced by the body through the breakdown of more complex carbohydrates during digestion.

Ribose is a type of sugar and a component of RNA (ribonucleic acid), which is one of the two major types of nucleic acids found in cells. The other major nucleic acid is DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid). Ribose is a pentose sugar, meaning it has five carbon atoms in its molecular structure.

questions.text: Which molecular structure of enzyme is essential for activity of enzyme?

options.explanation: A linear polypeptide with a specific sequence and number of amino acids is called primary structure. It is shown by all proteins at the time of their synthesis on ribosomal surface. After synthesis a protein does not remain in its primary structure but can be changed into some other structural conformations (particular form, shape or structure).

A helical (a-helix) or flattened sheets (13-pleated sheet) like structures which are established by H-bonding between opposite charge bearing groups of different amino acids are called secondary structures.
Some proteins exist in very complex structure in which more than one globule is attached together by hydrophobic interaction. Such structures are called quaternary structures.
In some proteins the linear polypeptide is changed into a-helix, then ahelix fold again and again by ionic bonds and disulfide bridges to form a globular shaped structure, the tertiary structure.

questions.text: The compounds which on hydrolysis yield polyhydroxy aldehyde or ketone subunits are:

Hydrolysis of polynucleotides involves the breaking of the phosphodiester bonds that link the nucleotides together. The products of hydrolysis of polynucleotides are nucleotides.

This option is correct. Hydrolysis of polysaccharides gives polyhydroxy aldehyde or ketone subunits. Polysaccharides are complex carbohydrates that are made up of repeating units of monosaccharides, which are simple sugars. During hydrolysis of polysaccharides, water is added to break the glycosidic bonds that link the monosaccharide units together. This results in the separation of the monosaccharide units from each other. The monosaccharide units that are produced are polyhydroxy aldehyde or ketone subunits.

questions.text: Which of the following type of carbohydrate has high molecular weight and is sparingly soluble in water?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Monosaccharides are the simplest form of carbohydrates, also known as simple sugars. They are the basic building blocks of more complex carbohydrates and play a crucial role in providing energy for cellular processes. They have low molecular weight and are soluble in water because they only contain 1 saccharide unit.
Disaccharides are carbohydrates composed of two monosaccharide units joined together by a glycosidic bond.

Oligosaccharides are carbohydrates composed of a small number of monosaccharide units (typically 3 to 10) linked together by glycosidic bonds. They are more complex than disaccharides but simpler than polysaccharides.

Polysaccharides are large, complex carbohydrates composed of many monosaccharide units (often hundreds or thousands) linked together by glycosidic bonds. They serve primarily as energy storage molecules or structural components in cells and organisms. Therefore, they are less soluble in water.

questions.explanation: Polysaccharides are made up of many monosaccharides subunits and have higher molecular weight as compared to monosaccharides and oligosaccharides. Polysaccharides are sparingly soluble in water while monosaccharides has high solubility.

questions.text: Bonds present in alpha helix are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In an alpha-helix, hydrogen bonds play a fundamental role in stabilizing the secondary structure. The alpha-helix is a coiled and compact arrangement of the protein backbone. Hydrogen bonds form between the carbonyl oxygen (C=O) of one amino acid and the amide hydrogen (N-H) of another amino acid, which is four residues ahead in the sequence. This pattern repeats along the entire length of the helix, creating a stable and regular structure.

Disulfide bridges are covalent bonds formed between two cysteine residues in a protein. They are responsible for stabilizing the tertiary and quaternary structures of proteins by forming strong, permanent links between specific amino acid side chains. While disulfide bonds are important for protein stability, they are not involved in the formation of alpha helices.

Ionic bonds, also known as electrostatic bonds, are formed between oppositely charged amino acid side chains. For example, if an amino acid with a positive charge (e.g., lysine or arginine) is in proximity to an amino acid with a negative charge (e.g., aspartic acid or glutamic acid), an ionic bond may form. While ionic bonds can contribute to the stabilization of protein structures, they are not a primary force involved in the formation of alpha helices.

Peptide bonds are covalent bonds that form between the carboxyl group of one amino acid and the amino group of another amino acid during protein synthesis. Peptide bonds link individual amino acids together to form a protein's primary structure. However, they are not directly involved in stabilizing the secondary structure of an alpha-helix.

questions.explanation: The alpha-helix is a very uniform geometric structure with 3.6 amino acids in each turn of the helix. The helical in structure is kept by the spiral formation of hydrogen bonds among the amino acids molecules in successive turns of the spiral.

questions.text: Sequence of amino acids is important in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The primary structure of a protein refers to the linear sequence of amino acids that are covalently linked together by peptide bonds. The specific order of amino acids in the polypeptide chain is crucial because it determines the protein's overall chemical properties, interactions, and function. Even a slight change in the amino acid sequence can significantly alter the protein's structure and function.

The secondary structure of a protein refers to the local folding patterns that result from hydrogen bonding between nearby amino acid residues. The two most common types of secondary structures are the alpha helix and beta sheet. The primary sequence influences the formation of these structures because certain amino acid residues have a greater propensity to participate in these hydrogen bonding interactions, based on their side chain properties and positions in the sequence.

The tertiary structure of a protein is the three-dimensional arrangement of the entire polypeptide chain. It is stabilized by various interactions such as hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, ionic bonds, and disulfide bridges between different amino acid residues in the primary sequence. The specific arrangement of amino acids in the primary structure dictates the folding and overall shape of the protein in its functional form.

Some proteins consist of multiple polypeptide chains (subunits) that come together to form a functional protein complex. The quaternary structure is the arrangement of these subunits and the interactions between them. The amino acid sequence of each subunit is crucial because it determines how the individual chains come together to form the functional protein complex.

questions.explanation: The primary structure of a princin is described by number, sequence and types of amino acids in a polypeptide chain.

The amino acid sequence of each subunit is crucial because it determines how the individual chains come together to form the functional protein complex.

questions.text: Which of the following is an unsaturated “fatty acid”?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is a saturated fatty acid. Butyric acid is a short-chain fatty acid with four carbon atoms and is typically found in small quantities in certain dairy products and butter. It is a saturated fatty acid because it contains only single covalent bonds between its carbon atoms.

This is a saturated fatty acid. Stearic acid is an 18-carbon saturated fatty acid, meaning it contains no double bonds between its carbon atoms. It is commonly found in animal fats, cocoa butter, and certain vegetable fats.

Oleic acid is an 18-carbon monounsaturated fatty acid with one double bond (a single point of unsaturation) between carbon atoms. It is one of the most common unsaturated fatty acids found in various vegetable oils, such as olive oil, as well as in animal fats.

This is a saturated fatty acid. Palmitic acid is a 16-carbon saturated fatty acid, containing no double bonds between its carbon atoms. It is one of the most abundant saturated fatty acids in nature and is found in various animal and plant fats.

questions.explanation: Butyric acid, palmitic acid and stearic acids examples of saturated fatty acids. Oleic acid is an example of mono-unsaturated fatty acid and has a double bond between C9 and C10.

questions.text: Starch is present in tuber, fruits and grains but absent in animal cells. Instead animals have a substance stored in liver and muscles known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Glucose is not stored. It changes into glycogen to be stored.

Glycogen is a highly branched polysaccharide and serves as a form of energy storage in animals, including humans. It is commonly found in the liver and muscle cells, where it functions as a readily available source of glucose when the body needs energy.

Galactose is a monosaccharide, a type of simple sugar, and is one of the essential dietary carbohydrates. It is chemically classified as a hexose because it contains six carbon atoms in its structure. Galactose is commonly found in various foods and is a component of lactose, the primary sugar present in milk.

Glucagon is a hormone produced by the alpha cells of the pancreas, an important organ involved in regulating blood glucose levels and metabolism. It acts in opposition to insulin, which is produced by the beta cells of the pancreas. Glucagon plays a crucial role in maintaining glucose homeostasis in the body.

questions.explanation: Glycogen is animal starch found in liver and muscle cell while starch is stored in plants cells.

questions.text: The covalent bond or bridge between two monosaccharides to form a disaccharide is called a:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A carboxylic bond is not involved in the formation of disaccharides. Carboxylic bonds refer to covalent bonds formed between a carbon atom and a carboxyl group (-COOH), which is present in certain organic compounds like carboxylic acids.

A hydroxyl bond is not a specific term and does not describe the type of bond formed between monosaccharides to create disaccharides. Hydroxyl groups are present in monosaccharides, but they participate in glycosidic bonds during disaccharide formation.

A hydrogen bond is a weak non-covalent interaction between a hydrogen atom and an electronegative atom (such as oxygen or nitrogen) in a different molecule. Hydrogen bonds are important in the stabilization of the three-dimensional structures of biomolecules, but they are not involved in forming the covalent bond between monosaccharides in disaccharides.

The correct term for the covalent bond formed between two monosaccharides to create a disaccharide is a "glycosidic bond." This bond is essential for linking monosaccharides together, creating various disaccharides like sucrose, lactose, and maltose. The type and position of the glycosidic bond determine the specific disaccharide formed and its properties.

questions.explanation: When two monosaccharides join through dehydration condensation process they form disaccharide with glycosidic linkage and release water molecules.

questions.text: Which one is an example of nucleotides?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is a nucleoside, a molecular compound composed of two main components: a nitrogenous base called adenine and a sugar molecule called ribose. It is a fundamental building block of nucleic acids, specifically RNA (ribonucleic acid), and also plays essential roles in various cellular processes as a signaling molecule.

Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is a molecule that serves as the primary energy carrier in living organisms. It plays a central role in transferring and storing energy required for various cellular processes. ATP is often referred to as the "energy currency" of the cell.
This is a nitrogenous base (purine).

NAD stands for Nicotinamide Adenine Dinucleotide. It is a coenzyme that plays a crucial role in various metabolic reactions within living cells. NAD is a derivative of vitamin B3 (niacin) and exists in two forms: NAD+ (oxidized form) and NADH (reduced form).

questions.explanation: Adenosine is a nucleoside while guanine is a nitrogenous base. NAD is a dinucleotide. Only ATP is a nucleotide among all options.

questions.text: The sum of all chemical reactions taking place within a cell is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Thermoregulation is the process by which organisms maintain their body temperature within a relatively narrow and optimal range despite changes in the external environment. It is a critical physiological mechanism that helps to ensure the stability of cellular and metabolic processes necessary for the proper functioning of living organisms.

Osmoregulation is the physiological process by which organisms control the concentration of water and solutes within their bodies to maintain internal osmotic balance and prevent excessive water loss or gain. It is a critical function for the survival and proper functioning of living organisms, as maintaining the right balance of water and solutes is essential for cellular processes and overall health.

Metabolism is the set of biochemical processes that occur within an organism to maintain life. It involves the conversion of food (nutrients) into energy, as well as the synthesis and breakdown of biomolecules necessary for cellular function, growth, and reproduction.

Isomerism is a phenomenon in chemistry where two or more chemical compounds have the same molecular formula but different chemical structures or arrangements of atoms. These compounds are known as isomers, and they exhibit distinct physical and chemical properties despite sharing the same number of atoms of each element.

questions.explanation: Word metabolism is derived from Greek word which means "Changes". questions.text: Which one of the following is an example of anabolic process?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Respiration is a fundamental biological process that occurs in living organisms, including plants, animals, and microorganisms. It involves the exchange of gases (usually oxygen and carbon dioxide) between an organism and its environment, as well as the release of energy from food molecules to fuel cellular activities.

Digestion is the process by which food is broken down and converted into smaller, simpler molecules that can be absorbed and utilized by the body. It is a crucial part of the digestive system, responsible for extracting nutrients and energy from the food we eat, and supporting various physiological functions.

Photosynthesis is a vital biological process that occurs in green plants, algae, and some bacteria. It is the process by which these organisms convert light energy from the sun into chemical energy in the form of glucose (a type of carbohydrate) while releasing oxygen as a byproduct. Photosynthesis is a key process in the ecosystem, as it is the primary source of energy for most living organisms on Earth.
Respiration and digestion are not anabolic processes.

questions.text: Most abundant organic compounds of a cell are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Water is not an organic compound.

Proteins make up around 10-18% of the dry weight of mammalian cells. Proteins are essential for various cellular functions, including enzyme catalysis, structural support, cell signaling, and transport of molecules.

Carbohydrates make up a smaller percentage of the dry weight, typically around 1-2% in mammalian cells. Carbohydrates play roles in energy storage (e.g., glycogen in liver and muscle cells) and as structural components (e.g., glycoproteins and glycolipids in cell membranes).

Lipids generally account for approximately 1-3% of the dry weight of mammalian cells. While lipids are essential components of cell membranes and play important roles in energy storage and signaling, their overall content is lower than that of proteins.
